is an electronic medium used for the recording, copying, playback, transmission, and display of moving visual images, with or without accompanying audio. Video technology was initially developed for live transmission and later expanded to include recording and storage through analog formats such as magnetic tape. Since the late 20th century, digital video has become the dominant form, enabling efficient compression, storage, editing, and distribution across broadcast television, physical media, and internet-based platforms. Advances in digital imaging, compression standards, and network infrastructure have significantly influenced media production, communication, entertainment, education, and information dissemination worldwide.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Electronic moving image

This article is about the storage and reproduction medium. For their content and production, see [Film](/wiki/Film "Film") and [Footage](/wiki/Footage "Footage"). For other uses, see [Video (disambiguation)](/wiki/Video_(disambiguation) "Video (disambiguation)").

A one-minute animated video showing an example of a media production process

**Video** is an electronic medium used for the recording, copying, playback, transmission, and display of moving visual images, with or without accompanying audio. Video technology was initially developed for live transmission and later expanded to include recording and storage through analog formats such as magnetic tape. Since the late 20th century, digital video has become the dominant form, enabling efficient compression, storage, editing, and distribution across broadcast television, physical media, and internet-based platforms. Advances in digital imaging, compression standards, and network infrastructure have significantly influenced media production, communication, entertainment, education, and information dissemination worldwide.

Video systems vary in [display resolution](/wiki/Display_resolution "Display resolution"), [aspect ratio](/wiki/Display_aspect_ratio "Display aspect ratio"), [refresh rate](/wiki/Refresh_rate "Refresh rate"), [color reproduction](/wiki/Color_reproduction "Color reproduction"), and other qualities. Both analog and digital video can be carried on a variety of media, including [radio](/wiki/Radio_broadcasting "Radio broadcasting"), [magnetic tape](/wiki/Magnetic_tape "Magnetic tape"), [optical discs](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc"), [computer files](/wiki/Video_file_format "Video file format"), and [network streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word *video* comes from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *video,* "I see," the first-person singular present indicative of *videre*, "to see".[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of television](/wiki/History_of_television "History of television")

### Analog video

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Analog video")]

See also: [Analog television](/wiki/Analog_television "Analog television") and [Videotape](/wiki/Videotape "Videotape")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/NTSC_Signal.png/250px-NTSC_Signal.png)](/wiki/File:NTSC_Signal.png)

[NTSC](/wiki/NTSC "NTSC") [composite video](/wiki/Composite_video "Composite video") signal (analog)

Video developed from facsimile systems developed in the mid-19th century. Mechanical video scanners, such as the [Nipkow disk](/wiki/Nipkow_disk "Nipkow disk"), were [patented](/wiki/Patent "Patent") as early as 1884, but it took several decades before practical video systems could be developed. Whereas the medium of [film](/wiki/Film "Film") records using a sequence of miniature photographic images visible to the naked eye, video encodes images electronically, turning them into analog or digital electronic signals for transmission and recording.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Video was originally exclusively [live](/wiki/Live_television "Live television") technology, and was first developed for [mechanical television](/wiki/Mechanical_television "Mechanical television") systems. These were quickly replaced by [cathode-ray tube](/wiki/Cathode-ray_tube "Cathode-ray tube") (CRT) [television](/wiki/Television "Television") systems. Live video cameras used an electron beam, which would scan a photoconductive plate with the desired image and produce a voltage signal proportional to the brightness in each part of the image. The signal could then be sent to televisions, where another beam would receive and display the image.[[3]](#cite_note-3) [Charles Ginsburg](/wiki/Charles_Ginsburg "Charles Ginsburg") led an [Ampex](/wiki/Ampex "Ampex") research team to develop one of the first practical [video tape recorders](/wiki/Video_tape_recorder "Video tape recorder") (VTR). In 1951, the first of these captured live images from [television cameras](/wiki/Television_camera "Television camera") by writing the camera's electrical signal onto magnetic [videotape](/wiki/Videotape "Videotape"). VTRs sold for around US$50,000 in 1956, and videotapes cost US$300 per one-hour reel.[[4]](#cite_note-4) However, prices gradually dropped over the years, and in 1971, Sony began selling [videocassette recorder](/wiki/Videocassette_recorder "Videocassette recorder") (VCR) decks and tapes into the [consumer market](/wiki/Consumer_market "Consumer market").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Digital video

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Digital video")]

Main article: [Digital video](/wiki/Digital_video "Digital video")

See also: [Digital television](/wiki/Digital_television "Digital television") and [Video coding format](/wiki/Video_coding_format "Video coding format")

Digital video is capable of higher quality and, eventually, a much lower cost than its analog predecessor. After the commercial introduction of the [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD"), in 1997, and later the [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") Disc, in 2006, sales of videotape and recording equipment fell. Advances in [computer](/wiki/Computer "Computer") technology allow even inexpensive [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") and [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") to capture, store, edit, and transmit digital video, further reducing the cost of [video production](/wiki/Video_production "Video production") and allowing programmers and broadcasters to move to [tapeless production](/wiki/Tapeless_production "Tapeless production"). The advent of [digital broadcasting](/wiki/Digital_broadcasting "Digital broadcasting") and the subsequent [digital television transition](/wiki/Digital_television_transition "Digital television transition") are in the process of relegating analog video to the status of a [legacy technology](/wiki/Legacy_technology "Legacy technology") in most parts of the world. The development of high-resolution video cameras with improved [dynamic range](/wiki/Dynamic_range "Dynamic range") and broader [color gamuts](/wiki/Gamut "Gamut"), along with the introduction of high-dynamic-range [digital intermediate](/wiki/Digital_intermediate "Digital intermediate") data formats with improved [color depth](/wiki/Color_depth "Color depth"), has caused digital video technology to converge with film technology. Since 2013, the use of [digital cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera") in [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") has surpassed the use of film cameras.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

### Frame rate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Frame rate")]

*[Frame rate](/wiki/Frame_rate "Frame rate")*—the number of still pictures per unit of time—ranges from six or eight frames per second (*frame/s* or *fps*) for older mechanical cameras to 120 or more for new professional cameras. The [PAL](/wiki/PAL "PAL") and [SECAM](/wiki/SECAM "SECAM") standards specify 25 fps, while [NTSC](/wiki/NTSC "NTSC") specifies 29.97 fps.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Film is shot at a slower frame rate of 24 frames per second, which slightly complicates the process of transferring film to video. The minimum frame rate to achieve [persistence of vision](/wiki/Persistence_of_vision "Persistence of vision") (the illusion of a moving image) is about 16 frames per second.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

#### Interlacing vs. progressive-scan systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Interlacing vs. progressive-scan systems")]

Video can be [interlaced](/wiki/Interlaced_video "Interlaced video") or [progressive](/wiki/Progressive_scan "Progressive scan"). In progressive scan systems, each refresh period updates all scan lines in each frame, in sequence. When displaying a natively progressive broadcast or recorded signal, the result is the optimum spatial resolution of both the stationary and moving parts of the image. Interlacing was invented as a way to reduce flicker in early [mechanical](/wiki/Mechanical_television "Mechanical television") and [CRT](/wiki/Cathode-ray_tube "Cathode-ray tube") video displays, without increasing the number of complete [frames per second](/wiki/Frames_per_second "Frames per second"). Interlacing retains detail while requiring lower [bandwidth](/wiki/Bandwidth_(signal_processing) "Bandwidth (signal processing)") compared to progressive scanning.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

In interlaced video, the horizontal [scan lines](/wiki/Scan_line "Scan line") of each complete frame are treated as if numbered consecutively and captured as two *fields*: an *odd field* (upper field) consisting of the odd-numbered lines and an *even field* (lower field) consisting of the even-numbered lines. Analog display devices reproduce each frame, effectively doubling the frame rate as far as perceptible overall flicker is concerned. When the image capture device acquires the fields one at a time, rather than dividing up a complete frame after it is captured, the frame rate for motion is effectively doubled as well, resulting in smoother, more lifelike reproduction of rapidly moving parts of the image when viewed on an interlaced CRT display.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

NTSC, PAL, and SECAM are interlaced formats. In video resolution notation, 'i' denotes interlaced scanning. For example, PAL video format is often described as *576i50*, where *576* indicates the total number of horizontal scan lines, *i* indicates interlacing, and *50* indicates 50 fields (half-frames) per second.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:2-11)

When displaying a natively interlaced signal on a progressive scan device, the overall spatial resolution is degraded by simple [line doubling](/wiki/Line_doubling "Line doubling")—artifacts, such as flickering or comb effects in moving parts of the image, appear unless special signal processing eliminates them.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[12]](#cite_note-:3-12) A procedure known as [deinterlacing](/wiki/Deinterlacing "Deinterlacing") can optimize the display of an interlaced video signal from an analog, DVD, or satellite source on a progressive scan device such as an [LCD television](/wiki/LCD_television "LCD television"), digital [video projector](/wiki/Video_projector "Video projector"), or plasma panel. Deinterlacing cannot, however, produce [video quality](/wiki/Video_quality "Video quality") that is equivalent to true progressive scan source material.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:2-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:3-12)

### Aspect ratio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Aspect ratio")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Aspect_ratios.svg/250px-Aspect_ratios.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Aspect_ratios.svg)

Comparison of common [cinematography](/wiki/Cinematography "Cinematography") and traditional [television](/wiki/Television "Television") (green) aspect ratios

In video, an [aspect ratio](/wiki/Aspect_ratio_(image) "Aspect ratio (image)") is the proportional relationship between the width and height of a video screen and video picture elements. All popular video formats are landscape, with a traditional television screen having an aspect ratio of 4:3, or about 1.33:1. High-definition televisions have an aspect ratio of 16:9, or about 1.78:1. The ratio of a full 35mm film frame with its sound track (the "[Academy ratio](/wiki/Academy_ratio "Academy ratio")") is 1.375:1.[[13]](#cite_note-:6-13)[[14]](#cite_note-:7-14)

[Pixels](/wiki/Pixel "Pixel") on computer monitors are usually square, but pixels used in [digital video](/wiki/Digital_video "Digital video") often have non-square aspect ratios, such as those used in the PAL and NTSC variants of the [CCIR 601](/wiki/CCIR_601 "CCIR 601") digital video standard and the corresponding anamorphic widescreen formats. The [720 by 480 pixel](/wiki/480p "480p") raster uses thin pixels on a 4:3 aspect ratio display and fat pixels on a 16:9 display.[[13]](#cite_note-:6-13)[[14]](#cite_note-:7-14)

The popularity of video on mobile phones has led to the growth of [vertical video](/wiki/Vertical_video "Vertical video"). [Mary Meeker](/wiki/Mary_Meeker "Mary Meeker"), a partner at Silicon Valley venture capital firm [Kleiner Perkins Caufield & Byers](/wiki/Kleiner_Perkins_Caufield_%26_Byers "Kleiner Perkins Caufield & Byers"), highlighted the growth of vertical-video viewing in her 2015 Internet Trends Report, noting that it had grown from 5% of viewing in 2010 to 29% in 2015. Vertical-video ads are watched in their entirety nine times more frequently than those in landscape ratios.[[15]](#cite_note-Constine-15)

### Color model and depth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Color model and depth")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/YUV_UV_plane.svg/250px-YUV_UV_plane.svg.png)](/wiki/File:YUV_UV_plane.svg)

Example of U-V color plane, Y value=0.5

The [color model](/wiki/Color_model "Color model") uses the video color representation and maps encoded color values to visible colors reproduced by the system. There are several such representations in common use: typically, [YIQ](/wiki/YIQ "YIQ") is used in NTSC television, [YUV](/wiki/YUV "YUV") is used in PAL television, [YDbDr](/wiki/YDbDr "YDbDr") is used by SECAM television, and [YCbCr](/wiki/YCbCr "YCbCr") is used for digital video.[[16]](#cite_note-:4-16)[[17]](#cite_note-:5-17)

The number of distinct colors a pixel can represent depends on the [color depth](/wiki/Color_depth "Color depth") expressed in the number of bits per pixel. A common way to reduce the amount of data required in digital video is by [chroma subsampling](/wiki/Chroma_subsampling "Chroma subsampling") (e.g., 4:4:4, 4:2:2, etc.). Because the human eye is less sensitive to details in color than brightness, the luminance data for all pixels is maintained, while the chrominance data is averaged for a number of pixels in a block, and the same value is used for all of them. For example, this results in a 50% reduction in chrominance data using 2-pixel blocks (4:2:2) or 75% using 4-pixel blocks (4:2:0). This process does not reduce the number of possible color values that can be displayed, but it reduces the number of distinct points at which the color changes.[[11]](#cite_note-:2-11)[[16]](#cite_note-:4-16)[[17]](#cite_note-:5-17)

### Quality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Quality")]

[Video quality](/wiki/Video_quality "Video quality") can be measured with formal metrics like [peak signal-to-noise ratio](/wiki/Peak_signal-to-noise_ratio "Peak signal-to-noise ratio") (PSNR) or through [subjective video quality](/wiki/Subjective_video_quality "Subjective video quality") assessment using expert observation. Many subjective video quality methods are described in the [ITU-T](/wiki/ITU-T "ITU-T") recommendation [BT.500](/w/index.php?title=BT.500&action=edit&redlink=1 "BT.500 (page does not exist)"). One of the standardized methods is the *Double Stimulus Impairment Scale* (DSIS). In DSIS, each expert views an *unimpaired* reference video, followed by an *impaired* version of the same video. The expert then rates the *impaired* video using a scale ranging from "impairments are imperceptible" to "impairments are very annoying."

### Compression (digital only)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Compression (digital only)")]

Main article: [Video compression](/wiki/Video_compression "Video compression")

[Uncompressed video](/wiki/Uncompressed_video "Uncompressed video") delivers maximum quality, but at a very high [data rate](/wiki/Uncompressed_video#Data_rates "Uncompressed video"). A variety of methods are used to compress video streams, with the most effective ones using a [group of pictures](/wiki/Group_of_pictures "Group of pictures") (GOP) to reduce spatial and temporal [redundancy](/wiki/Redundancy_(information_theory) "Redundancy (information theory)"). Broadly speaking, spatial redundancy is reduced by registering differences between parts of a single frame; this task is known as *[intraframe](/wiki/Intraframe "Intraframe") compression* and is closely related to [image compression](/wiki/Image_compression "Image compression"). Likewise, temporal redundancy can be reduced by registering differences between frames; this task is known as *[interframe](/wiki/Interframe "Interframe") compression*, including [motion compensation](/wiki/Motion_compensation "Motion compensation") and other techniques. The most common modern compression standards are [MPEG-2](/wiki/MPEG-2 "MPEG-2"), used for [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD"), Blu-ray, and [satellite television](/wiki/Satellite_television "Satellite television"), and [MPEG-4](/wiki/MPEG-4 "MPEG-4"), used for [AVCHD](/wiki/AVCHD "AVCHD"), mobile phones (3GP), and the Internet.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Stereoscopy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Stereoscopy")]

[Stereoscopic video](/wiki/Stereoscopic_video_coding "Stereoscopic video coding") for [3D film](/wiki/3D_film "3D film") and other applications can be displayed using several different methods:[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

* Two channels: a right channel for the right eye and a left channel for the left eye. Both channels may be viewed simultaneously by using [light-polarizing filters](/wiki/Light_polarization "Light polarization") 90 degrees off-axis from each other on two video projectors. These separately polarized channels are viewed wearing eyeglasses with matching polarization filters.
* [Anaglyph 3D](/wiki/Anaglyph_3D "Anaglyph 3D"), where one channel is overlaid with two color-coded layers. This left and right layer technique is occasionally used for network broadcasts or recent anaglyph releases of 3D movies on DVD. Simple red/cyan plastic glasses provide the means to view the images discretely to form a stereoscopic view of the content.
* One channel with alternating left and right frames for the corresponding eye, using [LCD shutter glasses](/wiki/LCD_shutter_glasses "LCD shutter glasses") that synchronize to the video to alternately block the image for each eye, so the appropriate eye sees the correct frame. This method is most common in computer [virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") applications, such as in a [Cave Automatic Virtual Environment](/wiki/Cave_Automatic_Virtual_Environment "Cave Automatic Virtual Environment"), but reduces effective video framerate by a factor of two.

## Formats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Formats")]

Different layers of video transmission and storage each provide their own set of formats to choose from.

For transmission, there is a physical connector and signal protocol (see [List of video connectors](/wiki/List_of_video_connectors "List of video connectors")). A given physical link can carry certain [display standards](#Display_standards) that specify a particular refresh rate, [display resolution](/wiki/Display_resolution "Display resolution"), and [color space](/wiki/Color_space "Color space").

Many analog and digital [recording formats](/wiki/Recording_format "Recording format") are in use, and [digital video](/wiki/Digital_video "Digital video") clips can also be stored on a [computer file system](/wiki/Computer_file_system "Computer file system") as files, which have their own formats. In addition to the physical format used by the [data storage device](/wiki/Data_storage_device "Data storage device") or transmission medium, the stream of ones and zeros that is sent must be in a particular digital [video coding format](/wiki/Video_coding_format "Video coding format"), for which a number is available.

### Analog video

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Analog video")]

Analog video is a video signal represented by one or more [analog signals](/wiki/Analog_signal "Analog signal"). Analog color video signals include [luminance](/wiki/Luma_(video) "Luma (video)") (Y) and [chrominance](/wiki/Chrominance "Chrominance") (C). When combined into one channel, as is the case among others with [NTSC](/wiki/NTSC "NTSC"), [PAL](/wiki/PAL "PAL"), and [SECAM](/wiki/SECAM "SECAM"), it is called [composite video](/wiki/Composite_video "Composite video"). Analog video may be carried in separate channels, as in two-channel [S-Video](/wiki/S-Video "S-Video") (YC) and multi-channel [component video](/wiki/Component_video "Component video") formats.

Analog video is used in both consumer and professional [television production](/wiki/Television_production "Television production") applications.

## Transport medium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Transport medium")]

Video can be transmitted or transported in a variety of ways including wireless [terrestrial television](/wiki/Terrestrial_television "Terrestrial television") as an analog or digital signal, coaxial cable in a closed-circuit system as an analog signal. Broadcast or studio cameras use a single or dual coaxial cable system using [serial digital interface](/wiki/Serial_digital_interface "Serial digital interface") (SDI). See [List of video connectors](/wiki/List_of_video_connectors "List of video connectors") for information about physical connectors and related signal standards.

Video may be transported over networks and other shared digital communications links using, for instance, [MPEG transport stream](/wiki/MPEG_transport_stream "MPEG transport stream"), [SMPTE 2022](/wiki/SMPTE_2022 "SMPTE 2022") and [SMPTE 2110](/wiki/SMPTE_2110 "SMPTE 2110").

## Display standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Display standards")]

Further information: [Display technology](/wiki/Display_technology "Display technology"), [List of common resolutions](/wiki/List_of_common_resolutions "List of common resolutions"), and [Broadcast television systems](/wiki/Broadcast_television_systems "Broadcast television systems")

### Digital television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Digital television")]

[Digital television](/wiki/Digital_television "Digital television") broadcasts use the [MPEG-2](/wiki/MPEG-2 "MPEG-2") and other [video coding formats](/wiki/Video_coding_format "Video coding format") and include:

* [ATSC](/wiki/ATSC "ATSC") – United States, [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea")
* [Digital Video Broadcasting](/wiki/Digital_Video_Broadcasting "Digital Video Broadcasting") (DVB) – [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe")
* [ISDB](/wiki/ISDB "ISDB") – [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan")
  + [ISDB-Tb](/wiki/ISDB-Tb "ISDB-Tb") – uses the MPEG-4 video coding format – [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina")

### Analog television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Analog television")]

[Analog television](/wiki/Analog_television "Analog television") broadcast standards include:

* [Field-sequential color system](/wiki/Field-sequential_color_system "Field-sequential color system") (FCS) – US, Russia; obsolete
* [Multiplexed Analogue Components](/wiki/Multiplexed_Analogue_Components "Multiplexed Analogue Components") (MAC) – Europe; obsolete
* [Multiple sub-Nyquist sampling encoding](/wiki/Multiple_sub-Nyquist_sampling_encoding "Multiple sub-Nyquist sampling encoding") (MUSE) – Japan
* [NTSC](/wiki/NTSC "NTSC") – [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan")
  + [EDTV-II Clear-Vision](/wiki/Clear-Vision "Clear-Vision") - NTSC extension, Japan
* [PAL](/wiki/PAL "PAL") – [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania")
  + [PAL-M](/wiki/PAL-M "PAL-M") – PAL variation, [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil")
  + [PAL-N](/wiki/PAL-N "PAL-N") – PAL variation, [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay") and [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay")
  + [PALplus](/wiki/PALplus "PALplus") – PAL extension, [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe")
* [RS-343](/wiki/RS-343 "RS-343") (military)
* [SECAM](/wiki/SECAM "SECAM") – [France](/wiki/France "France"), former [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), [Central Africa](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa")
* [CCIR System A](/wiki/CCIR_System_A "CCIR System A")
* [CCIR System B](/wiki/CCIR_System_B "CCIR System B")
* [CCIR System G](/wiki/CCIR_System_G "CCIR System G")
* [CCIR System H](/wiki/CCIR_System_H "CCIR System H")
* [CCIR System I](/wiki/CCIR_System_I "CCIR System I")
* [CCIR System M](/wiki/CCIR_System_M "CCIR System M")

An analog video format consists of more information than the visible content of the frame. Preceding and following the image are lines and pixels containing metadata and synchronization information. This surrounding margin is known as a *blanking interval* or *blanking region*; the horizontal and vertical [front porch and back porch](/wiki/Analog_television#Structure_of_a_video_signal "Analog television") are the building blocks of the blanking interval.

### Computer displays

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Computer displays")]

[Computer display standards](/wiki/Computer_display_standard "Computer display standard") specify a combination of aspect ratio, display size, display resolution, color depth, and refresh rate. A [list of common resolutions](/wiki/List_of_common_resolutions "List of common resolutions") is available.

## Digital encoding formats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Digital encoding formats")]

See also: [Video codec](/wiki/Video_codec "Video codec") and [List of codecs](/wiki/List_of_codecs "List of codecs")

A video codec is [software](/wiki/Software "Software") or [hardware](/wiki/Computer_hardware "Computer hardware") that [compresses](/wiki/Data_compression "Data compression") and [decompresses](/wiki/Uncompressed_video "Uncompressed video") [digital video](/wiki/Digital_video "Digital video"). In the context of video compression, *[codec](/wiki/Codec "Codec")* is a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of *encoder* and *decoder*, while a device that only compresses is typically called an *[encoder](/wiki/Encoder_(digital) "Encoder (digital)")*, and one that only decompresses is a *decoder*. The compressed data format usually conforms to a standard [video coding format](/wiki/Video_coding_format "Video coding format"). The compression is typically [lossy](/wiki/Lossy "Lossy"), meaning that the compressed video lacks some information present in the original video. A consequence of this is that decompressed video has lower quality than the original, uncompressed video because there is insufficient information to accurately reconstruct the original video.[[27]](#cite_note-Ghanbari-27)

* [CCIR 601](/wiki/CCIR_601 "CCIR 601") ([ITU-T](/wiki/ITU-T "ITU-T"))
* [H.261](/wiki/H.261 "H.261") ([ITU-T](/wiki/ITU-T "ITU-T"))
* [H.263](/wiki/H.263 "H.263") ([ITU-T](/wiki/ITU-T "ITU-T"))
* [H.264/MPEG-4 AVC](/wiki/H.264/MPEG-4_AVC "H.264/MPEG-4 AVC") ([ITU-T](/wiki/ITU-T "ITU-T") + [ISO](/wiki/ISO "ISO"))
* [H.265](/wiki/H.265 "H.265")
